data.title: U-Haul: Needs A Lot Of Work On The Profitability Side Of Things
data.description:
It’s been over 3 years since the first time I covered U-Haul (UHAL), and since then, the company’s share price has lost around 12% in value against SPY’s (SPY) 70% gain, so you would have been better off buying the broader index and sleeping much better than having UHAL in your portfolio. I wanted to go over its performance since then and see whether it is a good time to jump in. Spoiler alert, it is not.

Briefly on Performance

Starting from the top, we can see UHAUL’s revenues took a plunge from mid-’23 to mid-’24 and have been on a decent recovery ever since. The company experienced several weak quarters in 2022-2023, but then self-moving equipment rental finally returned to growth in fiscal 2024-2025. Self-storage has been a steady positive contributor, growing at around 9%-12% per quarter, with a little bit of a deceleration lately. Additionally, U-Haul has been opening and expanding facilities aggressively, with the portfolio reaching over one million storage units across North America, and as of Q3’26, the company added 5.9m net rentable square feet of new storage.

If we take a look at the most recent quarter, Q3’26, we can see that the top-line performance has been mixed: self-moving and equipment barely inching up, while self-storage revenues grew a respectable 7.9%. The rest are too small to list, so here is the overall performance below.

From these numbers, we can see that the growth came from a mix of some improvements in self-moving equipment rental revenues and self-storage revenues, but I would say it is mostly due to the company’s aggressive expansion efforts that drove growth in the top line over the last 1-2 years.

Moving on to the company’s efficiency and profitability, we can see a completely different story compared to its top-line performance. Margins across the board have been on a significant downtrend in the last three years, and it doesn’t look like there is going to be a recovery any time soon. This is because UHAL has a lot of fixed costs, like fleet, interest, labor, and liability, on top of quite modest revenue growth over the last while, with depreciation and weaker gains on truck disposal playing a huge role.

With the aggressive expansion of new locations comes added depreciation expenses, and since new locations usually start at low occupancy, this increases the company’s operating expenses and depresses margins significantly. We can see how much depreciation expenses grew in the latest quarter compared to the same quarter last year, below. An increase of around 20%, and around 32% increase in total.

Let’s take a look at the company’s financial position as of the latest quarter. UHAL had around $1B in cash and equivalents against $8B in debt. That is a big gap, but is it worrisome? The company is quite capital-intensive due to its real-estate-heavy business, so $8B in debt is not necessarily automatically alarming, but when we see that the company’s operations continue to decline, it could become a lot riskier if the company is not able to cover the interest expenses on the debt. And that is exactly what we see in the latest quarter. Due to the much higher depreciation expense, earnings from operations were a lot lower than at the same time last year ($33.6m vs $150m), and with this amount of debt, the company is paying $95m per quarter in interest expense, making the company unprofitable. It made $37m in net loss on a GAAP basis.

That is not a good outcome for the company. Clearly, operations are not going as well as expected. However, with $1B in cash and $1.3B in cash from operations, I’m sure the company is not going to default any time soon. But it is worth keeping an eye out in case there is further deterioration in operations.

Overall, I am not a fan of where the company’s performance has headed since my first article on it back in 2023, and I am not surprised that its share price underperformed compared to the broader market index.

Comments on the Outlook

So what is the company doing to get out of this rut and turn its operations right back around? It seems that the company is looking to continue to expand its self-storage business going forward, as the management believes this is where significant revenue potential still lies ahead. This, in my opinion, is mostly just hyping up its operations, because I don’t see this happening over the next year. First of all, the opportunity is there, but it relies heavily on modeled projections and ideal occupancy rates. In Q1 ’26, the company said that there is a potential for additional revenue of $261m from non-same-store locations, but the caveat is that this is only achievable if occupancy rates stabilize at around 90%. At that time, occupancy was 66%, and looking at the most recent occupancy was around 72%, down from 78% at the same time last year.

The only way to reach such additional revenue is to improve occupancy rates or expand a lot more aggressively, but expenses are going to increase dramatically as well, which will continue to drive its profitability further down. So, the main issue I see is occupancy rates, not the expansion part. That’s the easy part.

One way the company is trying to increase occupancy rates is through an initiative called 1-Year Price Lock. This means that the customer is not going to be blindsided by frequent price hikes and hidden fees for the first year of renting out the unit. It’s a good idea that is meant to reduce customer churn and improve trust. It must be working since the company’s occupancy rate did improve 600bps from Q1 to Q3. The company is trying to raise occupancy through price discipline, demand targeting, and faster lease-up of new storage capacity instead of relying on discounting, which is never good for profitability.

The company is also leaning a lot more on its migration data to place and grow facilities where population inflows are strongest. This index helps to keep expansion where it matters the most, and I believe there is a lot of opportunity to implement some sort of AI into the tracking of the data to make everything even more efficient. For instance, the Growth Index already uses a large transactional dataset, and with the use of AI, that dataset could be made a lot more powerful if combined with other signals such as housing affordability, local job growth, web traffic, and regional seasonality. Based on these, AI could come up with a more precise forecast model of where to focus its expansion efforts to maximize occupancy and profitability.

Should you buy U-Haul?

I’m afraid I cannot yet recommend the company as a buy. The company’s share price recovered 22% in the last month, and I don’t really see the reason why. Fundamentally, nothing has changed. The company’s operations are still in a downtrend, and it even went negative in the latest quarter, which makes its debt situation a bit worse than before.

I would like to see profitability improving before I would consider the company worthy of an investment; therefore, I maintain my hold rating.

data.title: Warsh, Oil, And The April FOMC: The Case For June Rate Cuts
data.description:
There is a question that's been stuck in my mind since Kevin Warsh was nominated as the next Fed Chair by Trump earlier in January: isn't the market too confident that the Fed has no room to move on monetary policy?

In fact, that question came up many more times as the conflict in the Middle East began, and we are now nearing the April FOMC meeting.

Look, despite being bullish (a stance that I consider contrarian, as I discuss in this piece), I get the concerns. Headline inflation printed a very ugly number in March, oil has spiked, consumer sentiment looks dreadful, and rate cut expectations have been pushed further out. On top of that, there are no signs of the end of the war in the Middle East, even though the market has already priced that in.

However, my view largely differs from the prevailing mood. In fact, I argue that we may see as many as three rate cuts this year, as the data (when looking beyond the war noise) justifies rate cuts as soon as the June FOMC meeting.

I am fully aware that my view (and therefore, my positioning, as I remain fully invested) carries risk, especially after Warsh denied a political bias from Trump to cut interest rates. If the war doesn't come to an end in the next few months and Hormuz remains closed, I may have to reassess my view on rate cuts.

March SEP Embedded Easing This Year

Let’s first separate the noise from the signal.

The March Summary of Economic Projections placed the 2026 median federal funds rate at 3.4%. In other words, they expect one rate cut this year.

As a quick side note, bear in mind that the March SEP was released on March 18, right in the midst of the Middle East conflict, with Brent trading at $107 that day.

In fact, the FED members are expecting further rate cuts next year, with the median federal fund rate projected at 3.1% by the end of 2027.

Let me go back to the Brent oil spike for a moment. Higher oil prices are indeed inflationary. Just take a look at how different the inflation (headline PCE) projections were in December vs March.

However, and this is key, the Fed's favourite metric is core PCE. Please take a look at the expectaions for core PCE between December and March, and again, please bear in mind that the Brent was trading at $107 the day the March SEP was released.

In comparison, the forecast for core PCE was barely changed.

Let me be specific now. In December, the Fed was looking at 2.4% PCE inflation and 2.5% core PCE. By March, the estimates were 2.7% for PCE inflation and 2.7% for core PCE.

Yes, that's still above the Fed's 2% target. However, we haven't seen core PCE trading below the 2% target since the pandemic in 2020. Believe it or not, that's 6 years ago (time flies)!

It is my view that the Fed's 2% target is outdated and needs a revision, as the economy is in a supergrowth cycle. In fact, I wouldn't like to live in an economy where the Fed's 2% target is met simply because it would imply a slowdown in the economy. With the AI supercycled (let's call it a bubble), that ain't happening any time soon, in my view.

Speaking of growth, the same SEP raised the median 2026 real GDP growth projection to 2.4%, up from 2.3% in December. In fact, the 2027 GDP median rose to 2.3% from 2.0%.

With this GDP growth, inflation is unlikely to cool off any time soon.

As for unemployment, the path remained unchanged for 2026, with expectaions still at 4.4%.

The March CPI Report Flipped The Inflation Debate

I still remember the release of the March CPI report, as I began to deploy capital in the markets the day prior to the release. As a side note, I’ve been raising cash since early this year, after I jumped ship from tech during the market broadening.

The headline CPI in March was up a ridiculous 0.9% m/m, the highest since June 2022.

In fact, the energy index was up 10.9% m/m, the largest since September 2005!

I didn't blink at that chart. In fact, my main focus was on core CPI, which excludes the noise (i.e., energy and food).

The increase in core CPI was 0.2% m/m and 2.6% yoy.

In other words, as seen above, the 0.2% m/m increase is basically noise, which suggests that the hike in WTI and Brent has limited contagion beyond energy prices. In fact, shelter increased by only 0.3%, food was 0.0%, and food at home was -0.2%. As a side note, my remark on shelter inflation (i.e., "only") is mainly grounded on the chart below:

The 0.3% m/m change in shelter is noise.

To be precise, the 70 bps gap between core and headline CPI is mainly explained by energy, as gasoline alone accounts for nearly 75% of the headline increase. Interestingly, the monthly change in gasoline prices was the highest ever.

As for gasoline, many experts believe the impact of the increase in crude prices on the CPI will be temporary, with the bulk of it showing up over the next two months. Why?

In my view, it's simply because gasoline prices are behind the jump in headline inflation (75% in March, as I noted above). As seen below, gasoline prices tend to adjust pretty quickly to the WTI.

Therefore, as soon as the conflict in the Middle East is over, I think we may see the WTI down to the low $80s, dragging down gasoline prices and, therefore, headline CPI inflation.

Am I worried about headline inflation? Not really, as the Fed often looks at core (PCE) inflation to reduce the noise from the signal.

The Surveys Are Pessimistic

The title above makes me think that being bullish is a contrarian play. Yes, you read that right.

The latest (preliminary) release of the University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index suggests that sentiment is the lowest it has ever been.

Yes, yes, yes. I know the objections to those surveys, as many argue that there is a political bias. I prefer to think (maybe naively) otherwise and believe the survey is a real representation of the consumer sentiment in the U.S. As seen below, the surveys show a dire picture for the U.S. consumer:

That's why I come to the conclusion that being bearish is the obvious trade and being bullish is a contrarian move. I have yet to meet a retail investor who feels pessimistic and still buys U.S. equities.

Coming back to the surveys, the preliminary April University of Michigan year-ahead inflation expectations rose from 3.8% in March to 4.8% in April.

Here is why I think these surveys are mainly noise, even if they correctly represent the consumer expectations.

Take a look at the dotted black line above, representing year-ahead inflation expectations. Notice the spike last year after Liberation Day, followed by a sharp decline in the months after.

Sentiment moves with the current. It can look dramatic on the surface, but it rarely tells you much about the deeper flow.

A Low-Hire, Low-Fire Labor Market

This is important, as it may give Warsh labor-risk justification for rate cuts without recessionary layoffs.

The latest Employment Situation report shows that March nonfarm payrolls increased 178,000, while the combined January-February revision was 7,000 lower than previously reported.

The BLS stated in the report that payroll employment had changed little on net over the prior 12 months. Unemployment is now sitting at 4.3%, which is above the post-pandemic lows of 3.4% in April 2023.

The trend in unemployment over the last few years is on the rise, but it's not recessionary. In fact, in February this year, the JOLTS report showed gross hires at 4.85 million, the lowest since April 2020, and a hiring rate of only 3.1%.

This is not a surprise, given the layoff wave in tech. In my view, the post-pandemic hiring glut, combined with high CapEx plans and the need to show EPS growth, is driving the layoff spree in megacap tech. The latest headlines come from Microsoft and Meta.

In other words, even if the unemployment rate went down in March, I think the underlying trend is dovish, as companies are prioritizing earnings growth over keeping their headcount.

My View On The Interest Rates This Year

The market is expecting no rate cuts for the April FOMC meeting.

That's nothing new. In fact, as I've been arguing many times in my recent articles, the surveys show no rate cuts until next year (now December 2027 appears to be the earliest date for the next cut):

Call me crazy, but I have low conviction in the survey results above. In fact, I think the strongest catalyst this year is a change in perception from no rate cuts until the end of 2027 to two (and even three) cuts.

Here is my rationale:

  1. Fed’s own baseline in the March SEP already allows easing this year, with the median fed funds rate for end 2026 at 3.4%, below the current 3.50%-3.75% range.
  2. The March CPI showed an energy shock, not broad core inflation (i.e., limited contagion outside of gas prices, transportation services or airline fares).
  3. Labor appears to be softer than headline payrolls imply. Even though March payrolls rose 178k and unemployment went down to 4.3%, claims recently rose to 214k, suggesting a low-hire, low-fire labor market.
  4. When (not if) oil prices come down, headline inflation will likely fall quickly, as 75% of the March spike was driven by gasoline prices.

How about Kevin Warsh and the committee hearing this week?

Well, he noted the following remarks on whether he was asked by Trump (who floated rates as low as 1%) on lowering interest rates:

the president never asked me to commit to interest rate cuts ... he did not demand it ... the president never asked me to commit to any such thing nor would I do so

Ok. That clears out a major question on the Fed's independence that we all had in our minds for quite some time.

However, before I conclude this article, I decided to include a short excerpt from this week’s committee hearing below, as food for thought on how independent the next Fed may (or may not) be from the administration:

Warren: Mr. Warsh, did Donald Trump lose the 2020 election?

Warsh: We try to keep politics, if I’m confirmed, out of the Federal Reserve.

Warren: I’m just asking you a factual question. I need to know- I need to measure your independence and your courage.

Warsh: Senator, I believe that this body certified that election many years ago.

Warren: That’s not the question I’m asking. I’m asking, did Donald Trump lose in 2020?

Warsh: I’m suggesting to you in 2020 –

Warren: I’m suggesting you can’t answer that.

Warsh: – the Fed mandate cured your huge inflation problem and you certified the election. We need to keep politics out of monetary policy and monetary policy out of politics.

Ok, so here is the bottom line: I prefer to remain a contrarian (i.e., a bull) and be fully invested heading into the April FOMC meeting. I see two to three rate cuts this year, with the first one likely starting in the June meeting (i.e., Warsh's first FOMC meeting as Chair), as I believe the data warrants cuts this year.

data.title: Walmart: Speculative Valuation As Free Cash Flow Is Held Down By Immense Capex
data.description:
The stock market in 2026 has rallied to fresh all-time highs, but the market hasn’t drifted uniformly upward either. 2026 has been a story of winners and losers. Most tech stocks outside the chip sector are major decliners, while semiconductors, energy, and consumer staples companies have led the way.

Walmart (WMT), the retail giant, has taken advantage of this flight-to-safety mentality to cross a $1 trillion market cap for the first time. Its stock is up ~15% since the start of the year and nearly 40% over the past 12 months, adding hundreds of billions in market value along the way. We have to ask: Can we rationalize Walmart’s gains?

I’m initiating Walmart at a "Sell" rating. Though it’s difficult to argue with the strength of this retailer’s execution in an incredibly tough economic backdrop, it’s also easy to see that this has become an overcrowded trade. There are overlooked aspects that make the bull case for Walmart weaker, and in particular, I’m eyeing the fact that after accounting for huge recent capex spending, Walmart’s earnings from a free cash flow basis are lower than the EPS denominators used in an already-rich P/E multiple.

The next major catalyst for Walmart is its Q1 earnings print, due on May 21. Take the opportunity of the recent rally to de-risk your Walmart position before then.

The positive highlights: Strong comps, rising gross margins

First, we should address exactly what has driven the bullish wave for Walmart in the first place. In a market environment that has been incredibly volatile, especially over the impacts of AI on layoffs and tech, Walmart has screamed stability and safety. The snapshot below showcases the company’s rolling four-quarter trend in comparable sales, or revenue growth from existing stores (excluding new openings).

In Q4 (the most recent quarter ending in January 2026), Walmart’s comps excluding the volatile fuel category grew 4.6% y/y, actually accelerating 10bps versus the previous Q3. This contrasts against deceleration in many retail peers, with many rivals pinning weaker sales trends on the tough macro.

Sales at Sam’s Club also maintained strength at 4.0% comps, despite decelerating from 5%-6% growth earlier in the year. We note that the membership-only Sam’s Club, like direct rival Costco (COST), relies heavily on its membership fees for a large chunk of both sales and profit. Investors cheered Sam’s Club’s recent price increase in its basic membership tier from $50 annually to $60, which will be an accelerating growth driver for FY27.

Walmart’s management has attributed the strength to its focus on everyday value, which has appealed to consumers across many income demographics in the tightening macro. Per CEO John Furner’s remarks on the recent Q4 earnings call:

Sales were strong across each segment of the business, and this includes sales of general merchandise, which grew on a global basis and was up low single digits for Walmart U.S. led by fashion. Fashion was a bright spot for us both in-store and online. Driving a healthier mix of sales growth in our business is important to the strategy overall, so I'm pleased with the trends we're seeing.

Across our customer base, spending continues to be resilient. In the U.S., we see the customer is choiceful in their spending. Again, this quarter, the majority of our share gains came from households making more than $100,000. For households earning below $50,000, we continue to see that wallets are stretched. And in some cases, people are managing spending paycheck to paycheck. That said, even these households are emphasizing convenience nearly as much as price.

We note that Walmart has managed a paradox of slight sales acceleration alongside slight gross margin gains, which is of paramount importance for a discount retailer. Gross margins in Q4 hit 24.0%, a 13bps y/y expansion. Again, this stands in contrast to many retailers who have decided to plunk down deeper promotions to retain market share in a declining economy. Walmart’s ability to grow revenue alongside expanding margins despite its value orientation makes it a true outlier, which has driven the buildup in premium for this stock.

Heavy capex, steep P/E multiples, and even steeper FCF multiples

Again, I won’t argue that Walmart’s sales growth and its execution have been admirable and make the company a true outlier in a largely struggling retail sector. Walmart deserves a premium over peers, but the question is, how much?

And before we get into the valuation discussion, we have to point out one thing that is largely overlooked when looking at simpler P/Es. Walmart is gaining in popularity not due solely to its retail merchandising and everyday low pricing, but also to its logistics capabilities as well.

For example, Sam’s Club just announced it was beginning to offer one-hour delivery for certain items in certain locations, which is what customers are getting in exchange for the membership fee increase. Walmart is giving Amazon Prime a run for its money in terms of convenience and speed, and that’s why the company is gaining solid sales momentum.

But we do have to step back and ask: at what cost? When we step back from Walmart’s earnings and margin profile, we find that its free cash flow has been hugely decimated by large capex spending. The snapshot below showcases the company’s cash flows: net income was $22.3 billion last year, and operating cash flows were $41.6 billion, but when we consider $26.6 billion of capex, free cash flow was actually $14.9 billion, 33% below full-year net income.

We do appreciate that Walmart has broken out its capex in more detail in its 10-K. The majority of the company’s $26.6 billion in spending lay in supply chain, logistics, and technology upgrades. Meanwhile, $5.6 billion went to existing store upgrades and remodels, while only $1.4 billion went to new location expansion.

Walmart spent 3.7% of its revenue in FY26 on capex. While the company has signaled that FY26 would be the peak investment year in terms of capex as a percentage of revenue, this isn’t necessarily true in nominal terms. For FY26, Walmart has moderated its capex % of revenue to 3.5% of sales. But the 3.75% midpoint of the company’s 3.5-4.0% constant currency sales guide implies $740 billion in annual sales, and capex at 3.5% of that sales profile is $25.9 billion - roughly in line with this year’s spending.

2026 has been the year when investors have scrutinized major tech companies’ data center capex. Oracle (ORCL), for example, has fallen sharply on its capex requirements to support OpenAI’s workloads. And yet we shouldn’t ignore Walmart’s capex intentions either. Walmart isn’t a software company with 80%+ gross margins and 40%+ operating margins at scale. The company’s plan to spend ~3.5% of revenue on capex, when operating margins sit at barely above 4%, takes a big bite out of earnings. We note that in FY26, Walmart’s capex spending in nominal terms was ~2x the $13.1 billion it spent in FY22, which was only 2.3% of sales at the time.

Okay, so where does this matter? The first is in valuation. Already, Walmart sits at multiples that could be considered highly speculative. Guidance for the current year, FY27 (ending January 2027), calls for $2.75-$2.85 in pro forma EPS this year, or 6% adjusted EPS growth.

At current share prices near $132, Walmart trades at a 47x P/E ratio, about double the broader S&P 500. Note that Walmart isn’t a growth company on the verge of a major technological breakthrough that will supercharge earnings growth. In no other normal market environment is a 47x multiple appropriate for a retailer with single-digit revenue and earnings growth. Walmart is only outmatched by equally frothy Costco:

Then we throw in the FCF factor. As previously mentioned, FCF conversion as a percentage of net income in FY26 was 67%. We expect slight improvement in FCF conversion for FY27 as FCF guidance squeezes down slightly to 3.5% of revenue instead of 3.7% in FY26, but the point remains: on an FCF multiple basis, Walmart’s multiples would look even more outlandish.

The other key point is that FCF limits Walmart’s capital return ambitions. In FY26, total shareholder returns were $15.7 billion, of which $7.5 billion were dividends (at current share prices, this reflects a poor <1% yield profile). Returns exceeded FCF of $14.9 billion last year, or a ~105% payout ratio.

A small chunk of Walmart’s EPS growth is driven by its aggressive retiring of shares, which it won’t be able to keep doing at its current pace without adding to its debt pile. In short, don’t ignore Walmart’s FCF, which is well below GAAP earnings.

Key takeaways

Again, don’t draw the wrong conclusions from my bearish thesis on Walmart. I think this is a fantastic, well-managed company that can go the distance and last for decades more, if not centuries. The company is making the right investments into its supply chain and store network to maintain its relevance with consumers and against Amazon.

But especially when we consider the scale of these investments, we cannot justify Walmart’s speculative valuation levels. It’s due for a correction (while I think oversold software stocks are overdue for the opposite direction, an upward re-rating). Steer clear of Walmart and wait for a correction before buying in.

data.title: AMD: You Haven't Seen Anything Yet (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
Previewing AMD's Upcoming Earnings

In my previous article on Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD), I wrote that the market was likely punishing the stock for the wrong reasons - back then, if you recall, AMD was falling sharply after announcing its Q4 earnings, despite sharing some positive commentaries. I'm glad I stayed bullish because time passed, and I see AMD trading over 50% higher when adjusted for the pre-market move-up at the time of this writing.

It might seem at first glance that the bullish narrative is going to fade out soon because the stock has gotten too pricey - even compared to its rival Nvidia (NVDA), which I'll demonstrate later - but in my opinion, that would be a faulty conclusion. AMD is rising for a very good reason that initially repriced the stock, and I think the repricing hasn't finished yet because the driver looks too bullish to fade in just a couple of months. The company is set to report its Q1 2026 results on May 5th, and I think it's very likely to deliver yet another exceptional quarter, beating the current consensus estimates. The valuation expansion is still ahead, in my view, so I keep my Buy unchanged ahead of the earnings print.

Why Do I Think So?

Initially, AMD was priced like a full-stack, vertically integrated AI infrastructure utility play in its niche, and as you may recall, AMD had been lagging NVDA for a long time, partly because its business remained over-diversified. In other words, the lack of specialization in data center revenues, along with exposure to declining segments like gaming, led to a significant missed opportunity in the GPU market, which has been red-hot since the launch of ChatGPT amid ongoing CAPEX scaling. If we open the five-year price chart, we'll see that despite the latest move-ups, AMD is still far behind NVDA in terms of its price returns:

Now, the narrative has shifted completely.

First, AMD's transition to the DC end market drove up the price and made the other end markets basically irrelevant for the entire growth story ahead. Higher specialization on what's truly driving up the business to date has helped AMD secure massive expansion. For example, in Q4 2025, we saw $10.27 billion in revenues (+34% YoY and +11% QoQ), with DC sales specifically rising by 39% YoY and now contributing over 52% to the consolidated results. Profitability also ticked higher, with the gross margin expanding by 290 bps in a single year (~57%), and even if we adjust it for the reserve release and specific China sales, it would be at 55%, so it's still very solid for a hardware company. The operating margin hit ~28%, which is similar to what some SaaS companies used to have. So, the change in the sales mix is shifting for the better, and the more DC contributes in the next few years, the better for the top line (and bottom line, too).

Second, in my understanding, AMD started to incentivize its biggest clients to aggressively dismantle Nvidia's CUDA software moat. What I mean by that is AMD's active deal flow - the deployment agreement with OpenAI (OPENAI) in late 2025, then the expanded strategic partnership with Meta Platforms (META) in February 2026. As part of the deals, AMD issued up to 160 million performance-based warrants to OpenAI and an additional 160 million warrants to Meta, with stock price thresholds scaling up to $600 per share. This is where these two key clients are incentivized - they become motivated to optimize AMD's open-source ROCm software stack and adopt AMD's UALink-over-Ethernet fabric, thereby providing AMD with more room for scaling and growth. Based on these findings, I conclude that AMD's top line will maintain its growth momentum over the next few years, as long as hyperscalers continue their CAPEX spending and execute on their budgeting plans. That's a clear win for AMD shareholders.

Third, the AI industry is going through a paradigm shift with agentic AI that requires systems to run continuously, handling multiple tasks in parallel. It's a massive increase in CPUs that control logic flaws, which weren't considered a bottleneck (as much as GPUs were in the first place) before. From what we see, this newly shaped tech trend has already caused an acute CPU supply shortage, pushing lead times from 1-2 weeks out to 8-12 weeks, and it's leading to price hikes from AMD and Intel (INTC). By the way, it's the reason INTC has grown so much on a YTD basis, leaving NVDA in the dust, figuratively speaking.

So, the current repricing drivers for AMD look secular, and that's why they're unlikely to stop next month or even next year. Recall how NVDA has been growing. I'm not suggesting that AMD will follow the same path or that NVDA is now out of favor. But AMD is no longer the "number two," as some investors were calling it just last year. It's just a different growth story now that should be supported by the end market's demand for the next few years at least, in my view.

As we look toward the Q1 2026 earnings print on May 5, we know that the management guided for $9.8 billion (+/- $300 million) in revenues, so it's going to be +32% YoY at the midpoint. Interestingly, the current consensus doesn't price in the higher end right now - we see that it's above the midpoint, but it's still ~2.22% below the high end, according to Seeking Alpha data.

The OPEX spending will grow in Q1, as the CFO noted on the call - AMD needs to support its AI initiatives like the multi-billion-dollar 6-GW contracts for Meta and OpenAI, and other stuff. I don't care about OPEX as much as the trends in the data center market - AMD should show us a QoQ sales expansion on that front, and if they do, it'll mean that the demand side is great because sales grew even in the historically weak quarter. I expect it to serve as a validation of the AI agentic demand flow. Plus, the management will likely give us positive comments on the call, the way INTC's management did recently. As a reminder, for the most part, both companies share basically the same growth driver in the CPU market (but AMD looks to be taking the CPU market share as it did before, thanks to its software layer superiority).

The management also guided for a Q1 GP margin of 55% on an adjusted basis, so it's going to be an important metric to monitor as well. We know that they're more about deploying entire liquid-cooled Helios racks rather than just selling standalone chips, so it leads to some margin compression. But still, assuming a multi-million-dollar Helios rack seemingly generates vastly more FCF than a 70% margin on a single discrete GPU, so I see no problem here at all in the longer run.

We need to hear more about the future plans, of course, such as the same Helios rack-scale ramp in 2H 2026. Functionally, their new MI455X is beating Nvidia's Blackwell and Vera Rubin in raw memory capacity, so once the production scale is set, I expect to see an avalanche of orders and subsequent sell-side revisions of AMD's top line for years ahead.

Speaking about AMD's valuation, I see that it's now valued at almost 2x the valuation of NVDA when we take only their forward P/Es for comparison.

So, yes, it's quite expensive (at least it's clearly more expensive than in the beginning of 2026 - see the chart above).

But it's not scaring me at all because the firm could easily beat forward projections. The thing is, P/Es are very sensitive to even small changes in operating leverage. Current earnings consensus implies 60% and 64% YoY growth for FY2026 and FY2027, respectively, but who knows what will come next year once the ramp-up is complete?

Analysts have modeled the current consensus based on previous selling prices, and I don’t think they had enough time to adjust their models after news broke that both Intel and AMD have started increasing selling prices for their products due to supply shortages and extreme demand.

Let's model the high end of today's FY2027 consensus EPS as the base one, which is $14.26 (see the table above). Okay, even if the actual EPS comes in at a 20% discount, we're getting ~$11.41 in EPS next fiscal year. If AMD keeps its P/E ratio at the 30-35x range for the level of future growth, we'd get an implied price target of $371/share. Following the pre-market move, AMD is at $341, so there's an upside of ~10%. But if the discount that I took above doesn't materialize and we see the high end turning out as the base case scenario, the implied PT comes in at $464/share in the next year or so.

Where Can I Be Wrong?

One of the biggest risks that few people talk about is the 320 million warrants issued to Meta and OpenAI that might lead to dilution ahead. For that to happen, AMD has to basically double, so it's a postponed risk, but still, there's a chance that AMD starts to scale through equity dilution.

There's also a risk that my out-of-consensus EPS estimate for FY2027 is simply too high. At 32-35x EPS next year, if the EPS comes in at the low end ($7.40), the implied PT would be ~$250/share. The consensus PT is a bit higher, but it's still below the current price, so I may be wrong in my undervaluation conclusions.

The Bottom Line

Despite the above risks, I like AMD even following its recent rally. I expect to see an outstanding earnings release in early May. Beyond that, I believe AMD's locked-in gigawatt demand provides great visibility into future earnings, so the management is likely to guide higher in Q1/Q2. I remain bullish on AMD, seeing an upside potential of 10-36%, depending on the FY2027 EPS figure.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Wynn Resorts: Betting On UAE
data.description:
Summary

Wynn Resorts (WYNN) is facing renewed competition within Macau, which could hurt margins. In addition, its highly anticipated UAE integrated resort, Al Marjan, which is expected to come online in 1H27, may not be a significant game-changing EBITDA driver for the company as a whole. We believe the share price is fairly valued at these levels. Forward EBITDA growth estimates are only at +1.6% vs. +5% for the sector average.

Wynn Resorts: global gaming operator

Wynn Resorts (WYNN) is a leading integrated gaming resort operator with two properties in Macau, two in Las Vegas, and one in Boston. Wynn's gaming properties are aimed at the higher-spending premium player in terms of providing luxurious hotel amenities coupled with high-end retail and food/beverage offerings. As a percentage of EBITDAR in 2025, Wynn's USA operations comprised 51% of the group's total compared to 50% of the total in 2024.

2025 saw EBITDAR fall in Macau and Las Vegas, mainly due to lower casino hold percentages, meaning players were lucky and won at a higher than typical theoretical rate. Management guided on the 4Q25 earnings call that Las Vegas EBITDAR would have been up flat to up low single digits yoy if normalizing these theoretical rates. Overall, I suspect that Wynn's gaming and hotel businesses remain healthy with positive volume growth.

EBITDAR USD '000 2024 2025 yoy
Wynn Macau 441,852 402,125 -9%
Wynn Palace 733,710 682,900 -7%
Total Macau 1,175,562 1,085,025 -8%
Las Vegas 946,762 902,405 -5%
Boston 247,128 236,721 -4%
Total USA 1,193,890 1,139,126 -5%
Total 2,369,452 2,224,151 -6%

LVS issued a warning sign recently

The premium gaming segment is the fastest growing in Macau, with all six gaming operators targeting the same class of higher spending customer. This results in margin pressure and lower earnings for the gaming operators. On April 23, LVS released earnings and specifically mentioned that the premium gaming segment remains competitive in Macau and could result in margin compression. LVS EBITDA margins in Macau fell by 1.4% pts yoy, and the stock fell by 8% in after-market trading.

Intense competition expected

Wynn opened a premium gaming area called Chairman's Club at Wynn Palace in February 2026. Chairman's Club expanded by 3x in terms of area to now be at 100,000 square feet and features additional food/beverage amenities for the higher end player. This new area resulted in strong gaming volumes and likely gaming market share gains, as evidenced by a recent report from Citi.

Our concern is that new capacity from Macau competitors will be introduced later in the year, all targeting the same premium player segment. MLCO will open its rebranded REM hotel in 2H26, formerly the Countdown Hotel, which has been closed since 2021 for renovation. REM will feature 150 suites with an average size of 1,000 square feet and have an avant-garde design. MGM Cotai will launch 124 premium suites in April. Galaxy Entertainment officially opened the luxury Capella Hotel in February. LVS recently said that new suites at its flagship Venetian property will open later in 2026.

Given that all of these properties (especially MGM and Melco) are within close proximity, Wynn could see market share loss as 2026 progresses. MGM, Melco, and Wynn's properties on Cotai are located adjacent to one another, which takes less than 5 minutes to walk among the properties. To combat market share loss, gaming operators could continue to engage in competitive marketing to attract players, which could place further pressure on EBITDA margins and hurt profitability.

Gaming growth is slowing in Macau

Total GGR (gross gaming revenue) for Macau grew by 9% in 2024 vs. 24% in 2023. YTD through March, GGR is +14%. Current consensus estimates for GGR growth in 2026 are around mid-single digits. In 2025, Macau's GGR per visitor fell by 5% yoy. If gaming growth is moderate this year, this means that the entire gaming market is not expanding significantly, which could place further pressure on operators to chase a limited premium customer base.

Moderate sector revenue growth is due to geopolitical risks leading to greater consumer uncertainty and slower economic growth from rising oil prices. In addition, prolonged economic slowdown in China, where the Chinese government forecasted GDP growth of 4.5% vs. 5% in 2025, coupled with a weak property market, slowing consumer consumption, and tariff uncertainty, is likely to rein in consumer spending in 2026, which could further hurt gaming revenues.

US operations are likely to be steady in 2026

We do not expect significant upside from Wynn's US gaming operations, as gaming volumes are likely to be flattish +/- a few percentage points. The US gaming market is relatively mature. Year-to-date through February, visitors to Las Vegas are flattish yoy, with convention visitors +3% yoy. Overall, Las Vegas hotel occupancy is down around 1% yoy, with REVPAR (revenue per available room) +4% yoy. Reasons for slower Las Vegas visitation include fewer international tourists plus weaker consumer confidence.

Not much upside from Wynn Al Marjan Island

Wynn has a 40% equity stake in Wynn Al Marjan Island, which will be the first gaming-integrated resort to open in the Middle East, with an expected opening in Spring 2027. The resort will feature around 1,500 rooms, in addition to a theater, 22 food outlets, shopping, and a beach club. The property is about a 50-minute drive from Dubai Airport.

Wynn is also planning a second integrated resort in the UAE on Al Marjan Island to be completed by late 2028. However, this property will be smaller in scale with only 132 rooms. Wynn management noted its UAE property could result in $465 million total in terms of EBITDA, which already takes into account the return of expats into the region following recent geopolitical events coupled with strong tourism growth (+5% yoy or 19.6m visitors to UAE in 2025).

We estimate the $465m guidance to be around 20% of Wynn Resorts total EBITDA. While I am positive on the earnings contribution, the 20% share of EBITDA is far less than MGM Osaka, which could potentially increase MGM Resort's total EBITDA by nearly 2x based on my view. The new property effect on earnings may not cause an MGM stock re-rating, but not Wynn.

Lack of catalysts - Hold ratings

Wynn will report Q1 2026 earnings in early May, where we expect strong volumes from Macau but will watch the EBITDA margins, which we believe could come under pressure due to intense competition. Given that no new gaming or hotel capacity at Wynn will come online in the near term along with margin concerns, we do not expect significant EBITDA growth for Wynn and believe the share price is fairly valued at current levels and maintain a HOLD rating. Consensus estimates for Wynn's forward EBITDA growth are only +1.6% vs. sector median at 5%.

Risks

Upside risks to our view are a stabilizing player marketing spend environment in Macau or stronger-than-expected market share gains in Macau. In addition, a significant recovery in Las Vegas gaming and hotel operations would lead to upside risks for the share price.

Conclusion

My main concern with the Wynn stock is the lack of clear earnings upside for 2026 (less than 2% EBITDA growth) given a stagnant US gaming market and a slowing Macau gaming market. These facts, coupled with the potential for lower margins from increased competition in Macau, along with Wynn Al Marjan not being a large percentage of future group EBITDA, result in our view that the share price could stay range-bound.
